nodes.name: Electric Utilities with Generation in Data Center Markets
nodes.mechanism: Data center electricity demand growth lifts load and drives higher realized power prices in constrained markets, increasing utility revenue and earnings.
nodes.logic: Utilities in regions with high data center concentration see demand growth exceed system averages, requiring new generation and transmission. Regulated utilities earn returns on capital investments, while deregulated generators capture higher energy and capacity prices. The demand surge is inelastic because data centers require uninterrupted power, amplifying revenue gains relative to demand growth.

nodes.name: Power Generation Equipment Manufacturers
nodes.mechanism: Utilities and independent power producers order new gas turbines, generators, and transformers to meet data center load, expanding backlogs and revenue for equipment makers.
nodes.logic: The scale of demand increase requires new dispatchable generation, primarily natural gas combined-cycle plants and peakers. Equipment manufacturers have limited production capacity, leading to extended delivery times and pricing power. A supercycle in orders lengthens revenue visibility and improves margins beyond typical cyclical upswings.

nodes.name: Transmission and Distribution Equipment Suppliers
nodes.mechanism: Grid upgrades to connect and support new data center loads require high-voltage transformers, switchgear, and conductors, expanding the market for transmission and distribution equipment.
nodes.logic: Data centers concentrate load in specific areas, often exceeding existing substation and line capacity. Utilities must invest in step-up transformers, GIS substations, and reconductoring. Equipment suppliers face multi-year order backlogs, allowing them to raise prices and expand margins. The need for rapid deployment accelerates demand for standardized equipment.

nodes.name: Hyperscale Data Center Operators
nodes.mechanism: Electricity is the largest operating cost for data centers; surging demand and constrained grids raise power prices and delay new capacity, pressuring margins and growth.
nodes.logic: Hyperscalers need massive amounts of reliable, low-cost power to maintain cloud service margins. In markets like Northern Virginia, interconnection queues and price increases erode the cost advantage. Additionally, securing enough power for expansion becomes challenging, potentially shifting capital to other regions with cheaper or more available electricity, but with higher latency or construction costs.

nodes.name: Commercial and Industrial Electricity Customers in Constrained Grids
nodes.mechanism: Grid capacity investment costs are often allocated across all ratepayers, so C&I customers in data-center-heavy regions face higher electricity rates without commensurate benefit.
nodes.logic: Utilities file rate cases to recover infrastructure investments for data center load growth, spreading costs to all customer classes. In deregulated markets, scarcity pricing from increased demand raises wholesale prices. Energy-intensive industries like manufacturing and logistics see input costs rise, hurting competitiveness in these regions.

nodes.name: Engineering, Procurement, and Construction (EPC) Contractors for Power Plants
nodes.mechanism: Utilities and IPPs increase capital spending on new generation projects, awarding large fixed-price and cost-plus contracts to EPC firms.
nodes.logic: The buildout of new gas plants and renewable-plus-storage projects requires experienced EPC contractors with electrical, mechanical, and civil engineering capabilities. A surge in project awards strains contractor capacity, allowing them to be selective and improve margins. Backlogs grow rapidly, providing revenue visibility for years.

nodes.name: Utility-Scale Renewable Energy Developers
nodes.mechanism: Data center operators seek to lock in low-cost, long-term renewable power purchase agreements (PPAs) to hedge volatile grid prices and meet emission goals, driving demand for new wind and solar projects.
nodes.logic: Hyperscalers are the largest corporate buyers of renewable energy, with long-term PPAs underpinning project financing. As grid power costs rise, renewable PPAs become even more economically attractive. The scale of data center demand creates a reliable offtake pipeline for developers, accelerating project development pipelines.

nodes.name: New Data Center Development Projects
nodes.mechanism: Grid interconnection delays due to equipment shortages and permitting extend data center construction timelines, increasing capital costs and delaying revenue generation.
nodes.logic: Securing transformers, switchgear, and transmission capacity often takes years, longer than building the data center shell. Developers face liquidated damages and lost customer commitments if power delivery is late. The mismatch between construction speed and grid readiness creates a bottleneck that raises the risk profile of development pipelines.

nodes.name: Precision Component Manufacturers for Gas Turbines
nodes.mechanism: Turbine OEMs increase orders for complex castings, forgings, and specialty alloys, benefiting suppliers with limited competition and high barriers to entry.
nodes.logic: Gas turbines require advanced materials like single-crystal superalloys for blades and large forged rotors. These supply chains are concentrated and capacity-constrained. As turbine backlogs grow, component suppliers gain pricing power and visibility, with deliveries scheduled years in advance.

nodes.name: Utility Equity Investors
nodes.mechanism: Political backlash from rising C&I rates leads to unfavorable rate case outcomes, tightening allowed returns on equity and limiting earnings growth for regulated utilities.
nodes.logic: When industrial rates spike, consumer advocates and large customers pressure regulators to cap utility profits. This can result in lower authorized ROEs, disallowances of prudently incurred costs, or mandates to absorb some grid costs. Utilities with heavy data center exposure may see valuation compression as growth expectations are tempered.

nodes.name: Behind-the-Meter Power Generation Providers
nodes.mechanism: Data center operators invest in on-site generation such as fuel cells, natural gas generators, or microgrids to ensure reliability and bypass grid congestion, creating demand for behind-the-meter equipment and services.
nodes.logic: To avoid dependence on the grid and hedge electricity costs, hyperscalers deploy on-site power. Fuel cell manufacturers and microgrid integrators see increased orders. This trend is reinforced by corporate needs for carbon-free energy, favoring technologies like solid oxide fuel cells with carbon capture potential.

nodes.name: Critical Mineral Mining Companies
nodes.mechanism: Renewable energy deployment accelerates demand for copper, lithium, and rare earth elements used in wind turbines, solar panels, and battery storage.
nodes.logic: Wind and solar installations are materials-intensive; a gigawatt of offshore wind requires significant quantities of copper and rare earths. Battery storage for renewable integration drives lithium and nickel demand. The supercycle in renewables for data centers adds to already tight supply, directly benefiting mining companies with established reserves.

nodes.name: Modular and Prefabricated Data Center Providers
nodes.mechanism: Delays in traditional construction push data center developers toward factory-built modular solutions that reduce on-site build time and bypass some grid interconnection complexities.
nodes.logic: Modular data centers integrate power and cooling systems in a factory, then ship for rapid deployment. They can be scaled incrementally, allowing developers to add capacity as grid power becomes available. This approach shortens time-to-market, making it attractive when grid delays threaten revenue timing. Providers of these solutions see increased demand for their integrated offerings.

nodes.name: Specialty Steel and Superalloy Producers
nodes.mechanism: Turbine component manufacturers increase purchases of high-temperature alloys like Inconel and specialized steels, benefiting mills that produce these niche materials.
nodes.logic: The complex metallurgy of turbine blades and combustion components requires advanced alloys with tight specifications, produced by a limited number of mills globally. As turbine order books extend, these mills see higher utilization and pricing. Long-term agreements with component makers lock in volume and price, improving revenue stability.
